title: Executive Order 9939—Creating a Board of Inquiry to Report on a Labor Dispute Affecting the Bituminous Coal Industry of the United States

flags.justification: The Board's powers and duties are defined directly by Title II of the Labor Management Relations Act (Taft-Hartley), 1947, not by presidential discretion — a narrowly statute-bounded investigatory body, not a seizure power.
A specific statutory reporting deadline (April 5, 1948) tied to the Act's own defined process.
An investigatory, fact-finding body; any subsequent injunctive action is a separate statutory step for the Attorney General, not this Board.
No compliance regime exists for an incentive structure to attach to.

The order explicitly states the Board 'shall continue in existence... until the Board is terminated by the President' after submitting its report — no automatic self-termination, echoing the same gap already identified in EO 10842's Taft-Hartley maritime Board of Inquiry.

This implements a new, congressionally-created structured dispute-resolution process specifically designed to replace the prior ad hoc executive-seizure approach (see EO 9393, EO 9728) — a positive, oversight-strengthening development.
A statutory investigatory process with defined next steps under the Act.

The first invocation in this corpus of the new Taft-Hartley national-emergency dispute mechanism, marking a historically significant shift from ad hoc presidential coal-mine seizure toward a congressionally-structured process, applied to essentially the same underlying industry dispute pattern seen in EO 9393 and EO 9728.
No second agency's competing mandate is implicated.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 2/12 = 16.7%. A directly connective finding to the seizure-order narrative already logged in this project: Congress responded to the 1946 coal strike (EO 9728) by enacting Taft-Hartley, creating this more structured Board of Inquiry mechanism to replace ad hoc presidential seizure authority. This is the first invocation of that new mechanism in the corpus, applied to a new round of coal-industry labor unrest — though the new mechanism inherits its own accountability gap (no automatic termination) rather than being unambiguously cleaner across the board.
